name: Dublin Castle
description: The seat of British rule in Ireland for over 700 years, Dublin Castle was transformed from a medieval fortress into Georgian state apartments. The Record Tower is the only surviving medieval tower.
history: Dublin Castle is a major Irish government complex, conference centre, and tourist attraction. It is located off Dame Street in central Dublin.

name: Maynooth Castle
description: The principal seat of the Kildare FitzGeralds, once the largest castle in medieval Ireland. Its massive keep was reduced by Henry VIII's forces during the Silken Thomas rebellion of 1535.
history: Maynooth Castle is a ruined castle in Maynooth, County Kildare, Ireland which stands at the entrance to the South Campus of Maynooth University. Constructed in the early 13th century, it became the primary residence of the Kildare Fitzmaurice and Fitzgerald family.

name: Castletown House
description: Ireland's largest and earliest Palladian country house, built for Speaker William Conolly in the 1720s. Its long gallery and print room are among the finest Georgian interiors in the country.
history: Castletown House, Celbridge, County Kildare, Ireland, is a Palladian country house built in 1722 for William Conolly, the Speaker of the Irish House of Commons. It formed the centrepiece of an 800-acre (320 ha) estate. The estate was sold in 1965, and later sub-divided.

name: Malahide Castle
description: Home to the Talbot family for nearly 800 years, Malahide is one of the oldest continuously inhabited castles in Ireland. Its oak-panelled rooms and botanical gardens sit just north of Dublin.
history: Malahide Castle, parts of which date to the 12th century, lies close to the village of Malahide, 14 km north of central Dublin in Ireland. It has over 267 acres (1.08 km2) of remaining parkland estate, forming the Malahide Demesne Regional Park.

name: Drimnagh Castle
description: The only Irish castle still surrounded by a flooded moat, Drimnagh features a restored great hall and medieval garden. Its Norman origins are visible in the tower and undercroft.
history: Drimnagh Castle is a Norman castle located in Drimnagh, a suburb of Dublin, Ireland. It is the only remaining castle in Ireland with a flooded moat around it; this moat is fed by a tributary of a local river, the Camac. Drimnagh Castle Secondary School is located next to the castle.

name: Dalkey Castle
description: A fortified town house in the medieval port of Dalkey, now a heritage centre with living history tours. It was one of seven castles that once defended this important trading port.
history: Dalkey Castle, formerly known as Goat Castle, is a medieval structure in Castle Street, Dalkey, Dublin, Ireland. The complex currently accommodates the Dalkey Heritage Centre, which is in the castle itself, and Dalkey Town Hall, which is formed by a single storey extension behind the original building.

name: Swords Castle
description: The medieval walled manor of the Archbishops of Dublin, Swords Castle features a pentagonal enclosure and chapel. Currently under restoration, it is one of north Dublin's most important medieval monuments.
history: Swords Castle is an early medieval castle located in Swords, County Dublin. Originally built for the Archbishops of Dublin in the early 13th century near the Ward River, some of the castle estate had fallen into disrepair by the 14th and 15th centuries. At least partially occupied through the 16th and 17th centuries, the castle was used as a place of rendezvous by Anglo-Irish Catholic families during the 1641 Rebellion. The site was afforded protection as a national monument and placed under the guardianship of the Office of Public Works in the early 20th century.

name: Monasterboice
description: An early Christian monastic site famous for its two magnificent high crosses, among the finest in Ireland. Muiredach's Cross features intricate biblical scenes carved with extraordinary detail.
history: The Monasterboice ruins are the remains of an early Christian monastic settlement in County Louth in Ireland, north of Drogheda. The ruins are a national monument of Ireland and also give their name to the local village and to a civil parish of the same name.

name: Mellifont Abbey
description: The first Cistercian monastery in Ireland, founded in 1142 by St Malachy with monks from Clairvaux. Its octagonal lavabo is one of the most distinctive medieval structures in Ireland.
history: Mellifont Abbey, was a Cistercian abbey located close to Drogheda in County Louth, Ireland. It was the first abbey of the order to be built in Ireland. In 1152, it hosted the Synod of Kells-Mellifont. After its dissolution in 1539, the abbey became a private manor house. This saw the signing of the Treaty of Mellifont in 1603 and served as William of Orange's headquarters in 1690 during the Battle of the Boyne.

name: Roche Castle
description: A spectacular Anglo-Norman fortress clinging to a volcanic crag in County Louth, its dramatic silhouette one of the most photogenic castle ruins in Ireland.
history: Built around 1236 by Lady Rohesia de Verdun, Roche Castle is a superb example of an Anglo-Norman fortress. Legend holds that Rohesia invited the architect to dinner after completion—then had him thrown from the window so he could never build its equal. The castle controlled a strategic position on the Louth-Armagh border and was abandoned by the 17th century.

name: Kilkea Castle
description: Ireland's oldest inhabited castle, a 12th-century Fitzgerald stronghold wrapped in 700 years of dynasty, rebellion, and legend — now a luxury hotel in the gentle Kildare countryside.
history: A motte and bailey was built here in 1180 by Sir Walter de Riddlesford. The manor passed to the Fitzgerald dynasty through marriage and remained in the family for over 700 years. Served as a military base for the Justiciar of Ireland in the 1350s. Substantially rebuilt by the 6th Earl of Kildare in 1426. Now operating as a luxury hotel.

name: Castledermot Round Tower
description: An ancient monastic round tower and exquisite high crosses standing in the quiet Kildare countryside, a Celtic treasure trove of early Christian art.
history: Founded as a monastery by St Diarmuid in the 9th century, Castledermot preserves a fine round tower, two remarkable Romanesque high crosses, and a Hogback tomb. The high crosses display scenes from the Bible including Daniel in the Lions' Den and the Crucifixion. The site was an important ecclesiastical centre and survived Viking raids.

name: Dunsink Castle
description: A modest medieval tower house in north Dublin, quietly decaying amid suburban encroachment, a forgotten relic of the Pale's defensive network.
history: Dunsink Castle is a small medieval tower house dating to the 15th century, located near the famous Dunsink Observatory in north Dublin. It formed part of the network of tower houses that protected the English Pale. Now in a ruinous state, it stands as a reminder of Dublin's medieval hinterland.

name: Howth Castle
description: An ancient seat of the St Lawrence family perched on the Howth peninsula, where 800 years of continuous habitation have created a fascinating architectural palimpsest.
history: Home to the St Lawrence family since 1177, Howth Castle is one of the oldest continuously inhabited residences in Ireland. The original Norman fortress was remodelled over centuries, with notable work by Sir Edwin Lutyens in the 1910s. Legend says Grace O'Malley, finding the gates locked at dinner, kidnapped the heir until the family promised never to refuse hospitality.

name: Bullock Castle
description: A squat Norman fortress overlooking the harbour at Dalkey, its weathered walls recalling centuries of monastic rule and fishing village life on Dublin Bay.
history: Built in the 12th century by the Cistercian monks of St Mary's Abbey, Dublin, Bullock Castle controlled the small harbour and fisheries. The castle served as a customs post and defensive position. It passed through various hands after the dissolution of the monasteries and stands today as a compact ruin in the seaside suburb of Dalkey.

name: Ardgillan Castle
description: A Georgian gem perched on Dublin's coastline with 194 acres of rose gardens, rolling parkland, and sweeping views across the Irish Sea to the Mountains of Mourne.
history: Built on land named 'Ard Choill' (High Wood) in Irish. The Taylor family (later Taylour) made it their home for over 200 years from the mid-18th century. The Down Survey of 1658 records it owned by wine merchant Robert Usher. Sold to German industrialist Heinrich Potts in 1962, then acquired by Fingal County Council in 1982 and opened as a public park.

name: Ardee Castle
description: The largest fortified medieval tower house in Ireland, this imposing Louth fortress now serves as a courthouse—justice dispensed from Norman walls.
history: Built in the 13th century and substantially enlarged in the 15th century, Ardee Castle is reputedly the largest fortified tower house in Ireland. The castle anchored the defence of the Pale's northern frontier. It was granted to the Pipard family and later passed to the Plunkett and Brabazon families. It has served as Ardee's courthouse since the 18th century.

name: King John's Castle (Carlingford)
description: A dramatic D-shaped Norman fortress perched on a rocky outcrop above Carlingford Lough, its massive walls framing sublime views of the Mourne Mountains.
history: Built around 1190 by Hugh de Lacy, King John's Castle at Carlingford is one of the earliest Norman fortresses in Ireland. King John himself visited in 1210 during his campaign to assert authority over the Irish lords. The castle features an unusual D-shaped plan and massive curtain walls, guarding the entrance to one of Ireland's best-preserved medieval villages.

name: Taafe's Castle
description: A striking fortified tower house in the heart of medieval Carlingford, its imposing bulk a testament to the wealth of the old merchant families.
history: Built in the 16th century by the Taafe family, this fortified merchant's house stands in the centre of Carlingford village. The Taafes were a prominent Anglo-Norman family who dominated local commerce. The castle features a fine vaulted ground floor and is one of several medieval buildings that make Carlingford a uniquely atmospheric heritage town.

name: Termonfeckin Castle
description: A compact 15th-century tower house standing guard over the quiet Louth village where early Irish Christianity once flowered.
history: Built in the 15th century, Termonfeckin Castle is a well-preserved three-storey tower house near the coast of County Louth. The village name derives from the Irish for 'sanctuary of St Feichin,' referencing the 7th-century monastery. The castle served as the residence of the Primate of Armagh.

name: Castleroche
description: A spectacular Anglo-Norman fortress crowning a volcanic crag in County Louth, with legends of its murderous lady builder and views stretching to the Mourne Mountains.
history: Built around 1236 by Lady Rohesia de Verdun on a dramatic rocky outcrop, Castleroche (also known as Roche Castle) is one of Ireland's most impressively sited Norman castles. Legend says Rohesia promised to marry whoever designed the castle, then threw the architect from the window. The castle controlled the Louth-Armagh borderlands and features massive curtain walls and a fine gatehouse.

name: Dunmahon Castle
description: A rare fortified tower house perched on the Louth coastline, its unusual coastal position making it one of the most scenic ruins on the Irish Sea.
history: Dunmahon Castle is a medieval tower house on the coast of County Louth near Blackrock. The castle dates to the 15th century and served as a defensive position guarding the coastline of the Pale. Its coastal setting distinguishes it from the many inland tower houses of the region.

name: Carlingford Mint
description: A mysterious medieval building in Carlingford village, one of the best-preserved examples of a 15th-century townhouse in Ireland.
history: Known locally as 'The Mint,' this striking medieval building in Carlingford dates to the 15th century. Despite its name, there is no evidence it ever served as an actual mint. The building features fine carved limestone windows and is one of several remarkable medieval structures that make Carlingford one of Ireland's most complete medieval villages. It was likely a wealthy merchant's house.

name: National Museum Collins Barracks
description: A vast 18th-century military barracks in Dublin, now housing the National Museum of Ireland's decorative arts, history, and military collections.
history: Collins Barracks (formerly the Royal Barracks) in Dublin was built in 1702 and is one of the oldest continuously occupied barracks in Europe. Renamed after Michael Collins in 1922, it was converted into a branch of the National Museum of Ireland in 1997. The museum houses superb collections of Irish decorative arts, military history, and cultural artefacts.

name: Rathfarnham Castle
description: A remarkable Elizabethan fortified house in south Dublin, whose austere exterior conceals exquisite 18th-century interiors of the highest quality.
history: Built in 1583 by Adam Loftus, Archbishop of Dublin, Rathfarnham Castle is an Elizabethan fortified house with four flanker towers. The austere exterior hides spectacular 18th-century interiors designed by Sir William Chambers and James 'Athenian' Stuart. The castle later served as a Jesuit college and was restored by the OPW. It offers guided tours of the remarkable interior.

name: St Audoen's Church
description: Dublin's oldest surviving medieval parish church, where a rare 12th-century font and lucky stone draw visitors to this atmospheric corner of the old city.
history: St Audoen's Church in Dublin's High Street is the city's only surviving medieval parish church still in use. The oldest parts date to the 12th century. The church preserves a 12th-century font, the 'Lucky Stone' (a 9th-century graveslab), and fine medieval tombs. The adjacent St Audoen's Arch is the only surviving gate of Dublin's medieval city walls.

name: St Mary's Abbey Dublin
description: The vaulted chapter house of Dublin's once-great Cistercian abbey, where Silken Thomas launched his doomed rebellion against Henry VIII in 1534.
history: St Mary's Abbey was Dublin's most important medieval monastery. In its chapter house in 1534, 'Silken Thomas' Fitzgerald dramatically threw down the Sword of State and renounced his allegiance to Henry VIII, launching a rebellion that led to his execution. The vaulted chapter house, rediscovered in the 19th century, is the only substantial surviving fragment.

name: Puck Castle
description: A medieval tower house in the Dublin suburb of Shankill, a surprising survivor of the old Pale's defensive network amid modern housing.
history: Puck Castle in Shankill, south Dublin, is a medieval tower house that once formed part of the defensive network of the Pale. The compact tower survives amid suburban development and is a reminder that Dublin's southern suburbs were once a contested medieval frontier.

name: Roodstown Castle
description: A remarkably well-preserved tower house in County Louth, its intact roof and original features making it one of the finest examples of its type in Ireland.
history: Roodstown Castle near Ardee, County Louth, is one of the best-preserved tower houses in Ireland, retaining its original roof, battlements, and much of its interior fabric. The 15th-century castle was part of the defences of the Pale and offers a rare authentic glimpse into medieval domestic architecture.

name: Oughterard Round Tower
description: The stump of an early Christian round tower in the Kildare village of Oughterard, near an ancient cemetery with a remarkable medieval church ruin.
history: Oughterard Round Tower in County Kildare is the stump of an early Christian round tower beside a medieval church ruin and ancient graveyard. The site is near Straffan and preserves early Christian heritage in the heart of the Kildare countryside. The round tower marks the site of an early monastery.

name: Athy Castle
description: A substantial medieval tower in the Kildare town of Athy, commanding the crossing of the River Barrow since the 15th century.
history: Athy Castle (also known as White's Castle) in County Kildare stands beside the River Barrow. Built in the 15th century by the Earl of Kildare, it guarded the river crossing. Athy was an important military position on the southern frontier of the Pale. The castle now houses a local heritage centre.

name: Clane Friary
description: Franciscan friary remains in the Kildare town of Clane, founded by a FitzGerald lord in the medieval heartland of the Pale.
history: Clane Friary in County Kildare was a Franciscan foundation. The town of Clane sits on the River Liffey in the heart of County Kildare, historically within the English Pale. The friary dates to the medieval period and was one of several religious houses in this area of strong Anglo-Norman influence.

name: Dunsoghly Castle
description: One of the best-preserved tower houses in Ireland, uniquely retaining its original oak roof timbers from the 15th century.
history: Dunsoghly Castle near Dublin Airport is one of the most important medieval buildings in Ireland—one of only a handful of tower houses to retain its original medieval timber roof. Built by the Plunkett family around 1450, the four-storey castle is remarkably well preserved. The roof timbers have been tree-ring dated to confirm their medieval origin.

name: Clontarf Castle
description: A grand castellated hotel on the site where Brian Boru won the Battle of Clontarf in 1014, breaking Viking power in Ireland forever.
history: Clontarf Castle Hotel stands on land associated with the famous Battle of Clontarf (1014), where High King Brian Boru defeated a Viking coalition but was killed in his tent after the victory. The current castellated building dates mainly to the 19th century but incorporates earlier elements. It now operates as a luxury hotel.

name: Ballyteague Castle
description: A tower house near the banks of the River Barrow in County Kildare, overlooking the wetlands and canal heritage of the Barrow Valley.
history: Ballyteague Castle in County Kildare is a medieval tower house near the River Barrow. The castle dates to the medieval period and overlooks the low-lying Barrow Valley with its rich ecological and canal heritage.

name: Morristown Latin Castle
description: A medieval castle in the Kildare countryside, its unusual name a mystery that adds to its charm among the rolling horse-country fields.
history: Morristown Latin Castle in County Kildare is a medieval tower house. The curious 'Latin' in the name may refer to a medieval school or religious association. The castle stands in the heart of Kildare's famous horse-breeding country.

name: Jigginstown Castle
description: The vast roofless shell of an unfinished 17th-century mansion near Naas—one of the largest brick buildings attempted in Ireland, abandoned before completion.
history: Jigginstown Castle near Naas, County Kildare, was begun around 1636 by Thomas Wentworth, Earl of Strafford, Lord Deputy of Ireland. The enormous brick building was intended to host King Charles I but was never completed after Strafford's execution in 1641. The massive shell—one of the earliest large-scale brick buildings in Ireland—stands as a monument to thwarted ambition.

name: Moone High Cross
description: One of the tallest and most remarkable high crosses in Ireland, its slender profile decorated with delightfully naive biblical scenes.
history: The Moone High Cross in County Kildare is one of the tallest high crosses in Ireland at over 5 metres. Dating to the 8th-9th century, the cross features charming, somewhat naive carved panels depicting scenes including Daniel in the Lions' Den and the Flight into Egypt. The distinctive granite cross is housed in the ruins of an early Christian monastery.

name: Old Kilcullen Round Tower
description: A round tower stump and high cross fragments on a hilltop in Kildare, site of an important early Christian monastery.
history: Old Kilcullen in County Kildare preserves a round tower stump and high cross fragments on a hilltop marking the site of an important early Christian monastery. The site offers wide views across the Kildare plains.

name: Drogheda Magdalene Tower
description: The dramatic bell tower of a medieval Dominican friary in the heart of Drogheda, one of the most striking medieval monuments in the Boyne Valley.
history: The Magdalene Tower in Drogheda, County Louth, is the 14th-century bell tower of a Dominican friary. It is one of the most prominent medieval monuments in Drogheda, a town with rich medieval heritage including St Laurence's Gate and several medieval churches.

name: Millmount Fort Drogheda
description: A historic motte-and-bailey fortification overlooking Drogheda, now a museum complex telling the story of this ancient Boyne Valley town.
history: Millmount Fort in Drogheda, County Louth, is a prominent motte on the south bank of the Boyne. The Martello tower on top was built during the Napoleonic era on the site of a much older fortification. The complex now houses the Millmount Museum, telling the rich history of Drogheda from prehistoric to modern times.

name: Moyglare Castle
description: A tower house in the Meath-Kildare borderlands, in the heart of Ireland's finest horse-breeding country.
history: Moyglare Castle is a medieval tower house in County Meath near Maynooth. The area is famous for thoroughbred horse breeding, with the renowned Moyglare Stud nearby.

name: Proleek Dolmen
description: A remarkable portal tomb near Dundalk with an enormous capstone balanced impossibly on tiny supports—legend says throwing a stone that stays on top grants a wish.
history: The Proleek Dolmen near Dundalk, County Louth, is a spectacular portal tomb dating to around 3000 BC. The enormous capstone (estimated at 40 tonnes) appears to balance impossibly on tiny point supports. Legend says if you can throw a pebble that stays on the capstone, your wish will come true.

name: Annagassan Viking Fort
description: The site of the first recorded Viking longphort in Ireland, where Norse raiders established a permanent base on the Louth coast in 841 AD.
history: Annagassan in County Louth is the site of the longphort of Linn Duachaill, one of the first permanent Viking settlements in Ireland, established in 841 AD. Archaeological excavations have revealed the extent of this important Norse base. The site is on the coast between Drogheda and Dundalk.

name: Kildemock Church Jumping
description: The 'Jumping Church' of Kildemock near Ardee—a medieval church ruin where one wall has inexplicably moved from its foundation, seemingly jumping inward.
history: The 'Jumping Church' of Kildemock near Ardee, County Louth, is a medieval church ruin famous for a wall that has mysteriously shifted from its foundation, leaning inward at an angle. Legend says the wall 'jumped' to exclude the grave of an excommunicate buried within the church. The phenomenon is likely due to subsidence but the mystery endures.

name: Kildare Castle
description: Norman castle remains in the county town of Kildare, famous for its cathedral, round tower, and ancient connection to St Brigid.
history: Kildare town preserves castle remains, a fine cathedral with a round tower that can be climbed, and the heritage of St Brigid, one of Ireland's patron saints. The town is also the heart of Ireland's thoroughbred horse-breeding industry.

name: Ballymore Eustace Castle
description: A tower house in the picturesque Kildare village of Ballymore Eustace on the River Liffey.
history: Ballymore Eustace Castle in County Kildare stands in a charming village on the River Liffey. The castle dates to the medieval period. The village's riverside setting and proximity to the Poulaphouca Reservoir make it a scenic spot.

name: Leixlip Castle
description: A fine Norman castle in the Kildare town of Leixlip, at the confluence of the Liffey and Rye rivers—one of the oldest inhabited castles in Ireland.
history: Leixlip Castle in County Kildare was built around 1172 by Adam de Hereford and is one of the oldest inhabited castles in Ireland. The town name comes from the Norse 'Lax hlaup' (salmon leap). The castle is in private ownership and was famously home to Desmond Guinness, founder of the Irish Georgian Society.

name: Clondalkin Round Tower
description: One of Ireland's finest round towers, standing in the Dublin suburb of Clondalkin—a remarkable early Christian survivor amid modern urban development.
history: The Clondalkin Round Tower in south Dublin is one of the best-preserved round towers in Ireland, standing 28 metres tall with its original conical cap intact. The tower dates to the 7th-8th century and marks the site of an important early Christian monastery. Its survival amid the modern suburb of Clondalkin makes it a striking anachronism.

name: Bremore Castle
description: A restored 15th-century tower house near Balbriggan on the north Dublin coast, with views across the Irish Sea to the Mountains of Mourne.
history: Bremore Castle near Balbriggan, north County Dublin, is a restored 15th-century tower house near the coast. The castle overlooks Bremore Passage Tomb, a Neolithic monument on the headland. The area combines prehistoric and medieval heritage in a coastal setting north of Dublin.
